items.q: Why does increasing the intensity of light below the threshold frequency not eject electrons?
items.correct: Each photon's energy is below the work function; many low-energy photons can't combine to eject an electron.
items.misconception: Enough low-frequency photons should eventually eject an electron if you wait long enough.
items.random_wrong: Low-frequency light doesn't have enough mass to push electrons out of the metal.
items.why: Students think energy is cumulative (more photons = more energy delivered), missing that each electron interacts with one photon at a time in the photoelectric effect.

items.q: What is the difference between the motor effect and electromagnetic induction?
items.correct: The motor effect produces a force on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field; induction produces an emf when flux linkage changes.
items.misconception: They are the same phenomenon described in different words.
items.random_wrong: The motor effect only works on DC; induction only works on AC.
items.why: Students see both involve coils, magnets and electricity and don't separate 'current causes force' (motor) from 'changing flux causes emf' (induction).

items.q: What determines the energy of a single photon?
items.correct: Its frequency (E = hf) — only frequency, not intensity.
items.misconception: A brighter light has higher-energy photons because intensity makes each photon more energetic.
items.random_wrong: Photon energy depends on how fast the photon is moving relative to the source.
items.why: Students conflate intensity (number of photons) with photon energy (frequency-dependent); this is why dim UV is more dangerous than bright red.

items.q: A 2 kg object accelerates at 4 m/s². What is the resultant force?
items.why: Correct answer: 8 N. “2 N” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What does it mean if the resultant force on an object is zero?
items.correct: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.
items.why: Correct answer: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.. “The object must be stationary.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Calculate the weight in newtons of a 5 kg mass on Earth (g = 9.8 N/kg).
items.why: Exact answer: 49 N. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
